sentiment.rationale: Nehemiah weeps, fasts, and prays upon hearing of Jerusalem's desolation, confessing the sins of Israel before God.
Artaxerxes grants Nehemiah permission and resources; Nehemiah inspects the walls by night and rallies the people to rebuild.
A detailed account of the families and groups rebuilding specific sections of the wall in organized cooperation.
Sanballat and Tobiah mock and threaten, but Nehemiah arms the workers and rallies them to fight for their families and God.
Nehemiah rebukes the nobles for usury against their own people and sets a personal example of selfless governance.
Despite plots and intimidation, the wall is completed in fifty-two days, and the enemies recognize God's hand in the work.
Nehemiah appoints gatekeepers and records the genealogy of those who returned from exile, mirroring the Ezra 2 census.
Ezra reads the Law to the assembly; the people weep in conviction but are told to rejoice because the joy of the LORD is their strength.
A national day of fasting and confession, recounting God's faithfulness from Abraham through the exile alongside Israel's repeated rebellion.
The people seal a covenant to keep the Law, promising tithes, Sabbath observance, and support for the temple.
Lists those who volunteer or are chosen by lot to live in Jerusalem, repopulating the holy city.
The wall is dedicated with two great choirs processing around it, and the joy of Jerusalem is heard from afar.
Nehemiah returns to find abuses (Tobiah in the temple, neglected tithes, Sabbath-breaking, intermarriage) and forcefully corrects them.

missingCharacters.name: Shemaiah
missingCharacters.description: A false prophet hired by Tobiah and Sanballat to frighten Nehemiah into hiding in the temple.

summaries.summary: Nehemiah receives word that Jerusalem's walls are broken down and its gates burned; he weeps, fasts, and prays a confession for Israel's sins.
King Artaxerxes grants Nehemiah permission, letters, and resources to go to Jerusalem; Nehemiah inspects the ruined walls by night and rallies the people to rebuild.
A section-by-section account of the families, priests, rulers, and craftsmen who rebuild specific gates and portions of the Jerusalem wall.
Sanballat and Tobiah mock and then conspire to attack; Nehemiah arms the builders, posts guards, and exhorts the people to fight for their families.
Nehemiah confronts the nobles and rulers for charging usury on their own people, and he sets a personal example by refusing the governor's food allowance.
Sanballat and Tobiah attempt to lure Nehemiah away and use a false prophet to frighten him, but the wall is completed in fifty-two days.
Nehemiah appoints gatekeepers, singers, and Levites for the city, and records the genealogical register of those who first returned from exile.
Ezra reads the Book of the Law before the assembly; the Levites explain its meaning, and the people are told to rejoice rather than weep, then keep the Feast of Tabernacles.
The Israelites fast, confess their sins, and the Levites lead a long prayer recounting God's faithfulness from creation through the patriarchs, exodus, and exile.
The leaders, Levites, and priests seal a written covenant to follow the Law, including promises regarding intermarriage, Sabbath, tithes, and temple support.
Lots are cast to select one in ten to dwell in Jerusalem; lists of residents, priests, Levites, and officials who settle in the city and surrounding towns.
Records the priestly and Levitical families; the wall of Jerusalem is dedicated with two great processional choirs, sacrifices, and great rejoicing.
Nehemiah returns from Persia to find abuses: Tobiah lodging in the temple, neglected Levites, Sabbath-breaking, and intermarriage, and he forcefully corrects each problem.

crossReferences.note: Nehemiah's opening prayer closely parallels Daniel's intercessory confession, invoking the covenant-keeping God.
Nehemiah echoes Moses's charge to not be afraid because the LORD fights for his people.
The joy of the LORD as strength parallels the psalmist's declaration that the LORD is his strength and shield.
The prayer begins with God as creator of heaven and earth, echoing the opening of Genesis.
Nehemiah references Solomon's fall through foreign wives as a warning against intermarriage.

verses.reason: Nehemiah sat down, wept, mourned, fasted, and prayed upon hearing of Jerusalem's ruin.
Nehemiah's declaration that the God of heaven will prosper them and they will arise and build.
Nehemiah exhorts the people to remember the LORD and fight for their brethren, sons, daughters, wives, and houses.
Nehemiah refuses to stop the great work to meet with his enemies on the plain.
The wall is finished in fifty-two days, and the enemies perceive the work was wrought of God.
'The joy of the LORD is your strength'—one of the most quoted encouragements in scripture.
Describes God as ready to pardon, gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness.
The joy of Jerusalem was heard even afar off on the day of the wall's dedication.
Describes God as ready to pardon, gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness.
